attributes.stem: A 7-day-old male infant born at 34 weeks' gestation by vaginal delivery with a birth weight of 1.5 kg presents with a one-day history of abdominal distension, vomiting and diarrhoea with decreased oral intake. He has been fed with breast milk since birth and has been receiving phototherapy for jaundice. He has no significant medical history. He has not had any recent fever or respiratory symptoms. On examination, he appears irritable but alert. His pulse rate is 150/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, and temperature 37.9°C. His abdomen is distended with diffuse tenderness. Rectal examination reveals blood-stained stools. A plain abdominal radiograph shows pneumatosis intestinalis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The patient has presented with a clinical and radiological picture that supports a diagnosis of necrotising enterocolitis (NEC). This is a serious inflammatory disease of the intestine that mainly affects premature infants. This condition is characterised by abdominal distension, vomiting, diarrhoea, feeding intolerance and widespread abdominal tenderness. Infants with NEC often have blood in the stools due to intestinal bleeding. It is an important cause of abdominal distension and tenderness in premature infants. Pneumatosis intestinalis is a radiological finding that is highly suggestive of NEC and is seen in about 50-70% of cases. This is consistent with necrosis of the bowel wall with infection from gas-forming organisms. NEC can progress quickly and lead to sepsis, shock and death, if not treated promptly.

Intestinal atresia is a congenital abnormality in which there is obliteration of the lumen of the intestine. It presents with bilious vomiting, abdominal distension and failure to pass stool. Intestinal atresia can occur anywhere in the intestine but is most common in the jejunum and ileum (combined) followed by the duodenum. Radiologically, duodenal atresia is characterised by a 'double bubble' appearance on a plain abdominal film. This results from the gas and fluid in the stomach and proximal duodenum. In jejunoileal atresia, multiple dilated bowel loops are seen with plain radiography. Intestinal atresia can be diagnosed prenatally or in the neonatal period and requires surgical intervention.

Hirschsprung disease is a congenital disorder characterised by the absence of ganglion cells in the distal intestine. The patient presents with constipation, abdominal distension, and vomiting. Hirschsprung disease is rare in premature infants and it is not associated with intestinal bleeding unless complicated by enterocolitis. Radiologically, Hirschsprung disease is characterised by a distended colon with a narrow rectum and hence is also known as congenital aganglionic megacolon.

Meconium ileus is an obstruction of the distal ileum caused by thick, tenacious meconium. It occurs mostly in infants with cystic fibrosis. Symptoms include abdominal distension, vomiting, and failure to pass meconium. Meconium ileus is usually diagnosed in the neonatal period and requires surgical intervention. Radiologically, meconium ileus is characterised by a "soap-bubble" appearance in the right lower abdomen with "microcolon" on contrast enema. The "soap-bubble" appearance results from the luminal gas interspersed within the thick meconium.

Volvulus neonatorum refers to twisting of the mesentery of the small bowel due to abnormal attachment of the mesentery to the posterior abdominal wall. The abnormal mesenteric attachment occurs during rotation and fixation of the gut during development. Symptoms include bilious vomiting, abdominal distension and constipation. Volvulus neonatorum with intestinal bleeding suggests bowel ischaemia. Early diagnosis is required to prevent bowel gangrene and can be reached by an upper gastrointestinal contrast study which shows an abnormal position of the duodenojejunal junction.
learningPoints: Necrotising enterocolitis mostly occurs in premature infants.
Pneumatosis intestinalis is a characteristic sign of necrotising enterocolitis.
references: Ginglen JG, Butki N. Necrotizing Enterocolitis. [Updated 2022 Aug 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-.
Jones J, Niknejad M, Hartung M, et al. Intramural bowel gas. Reference article, Radiopaedia.org (Accessed on 26 Jan 2023) https://doi.org/10.53347/rID-6168
Lin PW, Stoll BJ. Necrotizing enterocolitis. Lancet 2006;368:1271e83
